rule_text: Ensure maximal consistency between related derivative instruments by enforcing no-arbitrage constraints, using a model that makes minimal assumptions about true probability distributions.
entry_conditions: Detected arbitrage opportunity between digital option and call option of different maturities
Replication profit margin exceeds 0.5% of notional value
Model violates no-arbitrage constraint by >0.3%
exit_conditions: Model recalibrated to eliminate arbitrage
Arbitrage opportunity closes below 0.2% threshold
Switch to lower-assumption model (e.g., local vol over jump-diffusion)

rule_text: Ensure maximal consistency between related derivative instruments by enforcing no-arbitrage constraints, using a model that makes minimal assumptions about true probability distributions.
entry_conditions: Detected arbitrage opportunity exceeds 2.0% of notional value
Model permits profitable replication strategy between related instruments
Statistical evidence of Mandelbrot-style power-law or scaled distributions present
exit_conditions: Arbitrage spread closes below 1.5% threshold
Replication leg fails or becomes unexecutable
Model distributional assumption invalidated in market stress

A practical point that is more pertinent to a recurring theme of this

rule_text: A trader should account for volatility clustering and fat-tailed distributions when sizing positions and setting risk limits, because large moves follow large moves and markets exhibit extreme event risk beyond normal distribution assumptions.
entry_conditions: Historical volatility clustering indicator active (rolling 20-day vol > 50th percentile of trailing 252-day distribution)
Recent large move observed in past 5 trading days (|return| > 1.5 standard deviations)
exit_conditions: Stop-loss triggered at 3.5 sigma from entry
Volatility clustering indicator turns off (rolling vol < 50th percentile)
Position held for maximum 10 trading days regardless of profit

rule_text: A trader should account for volatility clustering and fat-tailed distributions when sizing positions and setting risk limits, because large moves follow large moves and markets exhibit extreme event risk beyond normal distribution assumptions.
entry_conditions: Historical volatility clustering indicator active (rolling 30-day vol > 40th percentile of trailing 252-day distribution)
Recent significant move observed in past 10 trading days (|return| > 1.0 standard deviations)
exit_conditions: Stop-loss triggered at 2.5 sigma from entry
Volatility clustering indicator weakens (rolling vol < 40th percentile)
Position held for maximum 20 trading days

rule_text: A trader should account for volatility clustering and fat-tailed distributions when sizing positions and setting risk limits, because large moves follow large moves and markets exhibit extreme event risk beyond normal distribution assumptions.
entry_conditions: Volatility clustering momentum present (rolling 60-day vol > 30th percentile of trailing 252-day distribution)
Multiple recent large moves in past 15 days (3+ days with |return| > 0.75 standard deviations)
exit_conditions: Stop-loss triggered at 2.0 sigma from entry
Volatility clustering momentum reverses (rolling vol < 30th percentile)
Position held for maximum 30 trading days

rule_text: Volatility exhibits mean reversion due to economic equilibrium constraints; a trader should position for reversion toward long-term volatility mean when current volatility deviates significantly from historical norms.
entry_conditions: Current volatility > 2.0 standard deviations above 100-day rolling mean
Historical volatility percentile > 90th percentile
Regime confirmation: not in structural trend
exit_conditions: Exit when volatility reverts to 1.0 std above mean
Exit on time stop at 20 trading days
Stop loss if volatility extends to 2.5 std above mean

rule_text: Volatility exhibits mean reversion due to economic equilibrium constraints; a trader should position for reversion toward long-term volatility mean when current volatility deviates significantly from historical norms.
entry_conditions: Current volatility > 1.0 standard deviation above 30-day rolling mean
Historical volatility percentile > 60th percentile

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2 (implied pp.13-210)
raw_chunk_text:
Finally, we could parameterize the risk-neutral distributions as in
Rubinstein (1998) or parameterize the implied volatility surface directly
as in Shimko (1993) or Gatheral (2004). Although these app
rule_text: A trader should avoid interpolating and extrapolating implied volatility surfaces in ways that introduce arbitrage opportunities (negative vertical spreads, negative butterflies, negative calendar spreads), as these violate no-arbitrage constraints and lead to unreliable local volatility estimates.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility surface is incomplete (sparse bids/offers)
Proposed interpolation method has not been validated against no-arbitrage conditions
Vertical spread value ≤ 0 detected in any interpolated segment
Calendar spread value ≤ 0 detected in any interpolated segment
Butterfly spread value ≤ 0 detected in any interpolated segment
exit_conditions: Do not trade; wait for complete observed surface data

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2 (implied pp.13-210)
raw_chunk_text:
Finally, we could parameterize the risk-neutral distributions as in
Rubinstein (1998) or parameterize the implied volatility surface directly
as in Shimko (1993) or Gatheral (2004). Although these app
rule_text: A trader should avoid interpolating and extrapolating implied volatility surfaces in ways that introduce arbitrage opportunities (negative vertical spreads, negative butterflies, negative calendar spreads), as these violate no-arbitrage constraints and lead to unreliable local volatility estimates.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility surface is incomplete
Interpolation method enforces no-arbitrage via convex optimization
Vertical spread constraint: all values > 0 by at least 1 bp
Calendar spread constraint: all values > 0 by at least 1 bp
Butterfly spread constraint: all values > 0 by at least 0.5 bp
exit_conditions: Observed market data contradicts interpolation by >2 bp
Any spread becomes negative in live market

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2 (implied pp.13-210)
raw_chunk_text:
Finally, we could parameterize the risk-neutral distributions as in
Rubinstein (1998) or parameterize the implied volatility surface directly
as in Shimko (1993) or Gatheral (2004). Although these app
rule_text: A trader should avoid interpolating and extrapolating implied volatility surfaces in ways that introduce arbitrage opportunities (negative vertical spreads, negative butterflies, negative calendar spreads), as these violate no-arbitrage constraints and lead to unreliable local volatility estimates.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility surface is incomplete
Direct parameterization (Shimko/Gatheral method) applied to sparse data
Vertical spread violations < 2 bp or detected violations neutralized via hedging
Calendar spread violations < 2 bp in extrapolated regions only
Butterfly spread violations < 1 bp
exit_conditions: Spread violation exceeds 2 bp
Surface parameterization error > 3 bp from observed quotes
Position marked-to-market loss > 1% of notional

Equation (3.5) expresses implied variance as the time-integral
rule_text: A delta hedger's profit in any time interval is directly proportional to gamma and the difference between expected instantaneous variance and realized instantaneous variance; therefore, position gamma should be sized relative to the forecast variance gap.
entry_conditions: σ_implied(K,T)² > σ_realized_forecast² × 1.5
Gamma > 0 (long gamma position available)
Time to expiration T > 14 days
exit_conditions: σ_implied drops below σ_realized_forecast × 1.3
Time decay reaches 7 days to expiration
Realized variance exceeds implied variance

rule_text: A trader should be skeptical of deep out-of-the-money option prices that imply tail-move probabilities orders of magnitude beyond normal distribution expectations, as these represent mispricing opportunities or liquidity compensation rather than true risk.
entry_conditions: Option is >10 standard deviations OTM based on current ATM vol and overnight variance contribution
Bid price exists (indicates liquidity)
Implied probability of required move exceeds theoretical probability by 10x or more
exit_conditions: Price decays to fair value or volatility surface reprices
Time decay reaches defined threshold (e.g., 50% time remaining)

rule_text: A trader should be skeptical of deep out-of-the-money option prices that imply tail-move probabilities orders of magnitude beyond normal distribution expectations, as these represent mispricing opportunities or liquidity compensation rather than true risk.

Bid exists regardless of spread width
Implied probability of required move exceeds theoretical probability by 3x or more
exit_conditions: Position reaches 25% max profit
Time to expiration <2 days

rule_text: A trader should bid for out-of-the-money options when large moves are economically possible, as a hedge against tail risk and existing portfolio exposure, because diffusion models systematically underprice extreme short-end moves.
entry_conditions: Portfolio delta > +0.6 (heavily long exposure)
Observed IV smile at 0.25 delta > Heston model IV by +15% or more
Days to expiration ≤ 7 days
Option moneyness (K/S) between 0.90–0.95 (put side) or 1.05–1.10 (call side)
exit_conditions: Large move occurs (>3 sigma) and option is ITM, or expiration
Delta of portfolio returns below 0.3

rule_text: A trader should monitor the characteristic time T* (where jump effects become detectable in returns) relative to time to expiration; when time to expiration falls below T*, jump risk becomes identifiable and should influence position management.
entry_conditions: Time to expiration > 2.0 × T*
Jump diffusion parameters estimated (λ, α, δ known or inferred)
ATM variance skew monitored
exit_conditions: TTX < 1.5 × T*: reduce position by 50%
TTX < 0.5 × T*: exit completely

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2, pp.65-66
raw_chunk_text:
Summarizing the results, we note that the jump compensator (or
expected move in the stock price due to jumps) drives the skew in the
short-expiration limit while the decay of ATM skew is driven by the
rule_text: A trader should adjust skew exposure and time-decay hedging based on the relative contribution of jump risk versus stochastic volatility, as jumps drive short-dated skew decay while volatility regime drives longer-dated skew behavior.
entry_conditions: ATM skew decay rate > 2.5x normal baseline (indicating jump dominance)
Time to expiration < 7 days (short-dated skew zone)
Jump compensator estimate > 75th percentile of rolling 60-day distribution
exit_conditions: Time to expiration > 14 days (skew reverts to volatility regime)
Jump compensator drops below 50th percentile

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2, pp.65-66
raw_chunk_text:
Summarizing the results, we note that the jump compensator (or
expected move in the stock price due to jumps) drives the skew in the
short-expiration limit while the decay of ATM skew is driven by the
rule_text: A trader should adjust skew exposure and time-decay hedging based on the relative contribution of jump risk versus stochastic volatility, as jumps drive short-dated skew decay while volatility regime drives longer-dated skew behavior.
entry_conditions: ATM skew decay rate > 1.5x normal baseline
Time to expiration between 5 and 21 days (medium-dated skew zone)
Jump compensator estimate > 60th percentile of rolling 30-day distribution
exit_conditions: Time to expiration < 4 days or > 28 days
Jump compensator drops below 40th percentile

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2, pp.65-66

Summarizing the results, we note that the jump compensator (or
expected move in the stock price due to jumps) drives the skew in the
short-expiration limit while the decay of ATM skew is driven by the
rule_text: A trader should adjust skew exposure and time-decay hedging based on the relative contribution of jump risk versus stochastic volatility, as jumps drive short-dated skew decay while volatility regime drives longer-dated skew behavior.
entry_conditions: ATM skew decay rate > 1.0x normal baseline (any acceleration signal)
Time to expiration between 3 and 60 days (full skew surface)
Jump compensator estimate > 50th percentile of rolling 14-day distribution
exit_conditions: Time to expiration < 2 days or > 90 days
Jump compensator drops below 30th percentile

rule_text: After large price moves in the underlying asset, implied volatilities increase substantially (jump). A trader should expect and position for volatility expansion following significant underlying moves, as this is an empirically observed property of asset returns that static models fail to capture.
entry_conditions: Underlying price move > 2.0 standard deviations in absolute value
Current implied volatility < 75th percentile of historical IV range
Time to expiration > 7 days
exit_conditions: Implied volatility reaches 90th percentile of IV range
Position duration exceeds 15 days
Underlying reverses by 1 standard deviation

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.6 (Implications for the Volatility Skew), pp.13-210
raw_chunk_text:
Implications for the Volatility Skew
Allissuersofstockhavesomeprobabilityofdefaulting.Thereisaveryactive
credit derivative market that prices default-risk (see Default-Risk.com for
background). Black-
rule_text: A trader should adjust implied volatility expectations and option valuation models when pricing options on high-credit-spread issuers, because the risky discount rate creates a downside volatility skew that standard Black-Scholes underestimates, particularly in short-dated options.
entry_conditions: Issuer credit spread > 300 basis points
Option time-to-expiry < 60 days
Observed implied volatility skew < model-predicted skew by >10%
exit_conditions: Credit spread compresses below 250 bps
Option expires or approaches 30 days to expiration
Skew converges to model prediction within 5%

rule_text: Exploit relative mispricing between equity options and credit derivatives by identifying when put options are cheaper than equivalent credit protection, then establishing a relative value arbitrage by buying the underpriced instrument and selling the overpriced one.
entry_conditions: Calculate put option price from ATM implied vol
Calculate equivalent credit derivative price from bond yield spread
Put price is cheaper than credit derivative by threshold amount
Mispricing spread exceeds 2.0x historical standard deviation
exit_conditions: Mispricing closes to within 0.5 std devs
Holding period exceeded
Credit event occurs

rule_text: A trader should exploit market maker heuristic-based mispricing by simultaneously buying relatively expensive options in one market while selling offsetting exposure in a related market when implied volatility skews deviate significantly from fundamentally justified levels.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility skew percentile > 95th (measured over 252-day rolling window)
Skew has persisted steeper than 90th percentile for ≥5 trading days
Bid-ask spread differential between exchange option and CDS market <20bps
exit_conditions: Skew mean-reverts to 50th percentile OR
Holding period exceeds 60 days OR
Mark-to-market loss exceeds 2% of notional

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch. x, pp. 13-210
raw_chunk_text:

rules to determine whether a skew looked reasonable or not; implied

rule_text: A trader should exploit market maker heuristic-based mispricing by simultaneously buying relatively expensive options in one market while selling offsetting exposure in a related market when implied volatility skews deviate significantly from fundamentally justified levels.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility skew percentile > 85th (measured over 252-day rolling window)
Skew has persisted steeper than 80th percentile for ≥2 trading days
Bid-ask spread on exchange option >2x normal regime for that strike
IV quote rejection (market maker revises price downward after brief wide quote)
exit_conditions: Skew mean-reverts to 60th percentile OR
Holding period exceeds 30 days OR
Mark-to-market loss exceeds 3% of notional

rule_text: A trader should exploit market maker heuristic-based mispricing by simultaneously buying relatively expensive options in one market while selling offsetting exposure in a related market when implied volatility skews deviate significantly from fundamentally justified levels.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility skew percentile > 70th (measured over 252-day rolling window)
Skew increased by ≥10% vs. previous day's skew steepness
Exchange option IV > CDS-implied put strike IV by >50bps
exit_conditions: Skew mean-reverts to 75th percentile OR
Holding period exceeds 10 days OR
Mark-to-market loss exceeds 5% of notional

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.6 (Modeling Default Risk), p.79
raw_chunk_text:
Ultimately, skews became so steep that the hedge funds made money

rule_text: Execute put spread arbitrage when skew creates a pricing opportunity where a multi-leg put spread can be traded at zero cost or for a net credit, locking in risk-free profit from the volatility surface mispricing.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility skew steepness: slope > 0.15 (vega-weighted change per strike octile)
Put spread net premium received: > +5% of ATM strike value
Credit default swap spread component: < 100 bps
Minimum payoff floor verified: lowest node in payoff diagram > 0
exit_conditions: Spread widens to flat or debit: exit immediately
Skew flattens below 10 bps: close position
Time decay threshold: close if extrinsic value < 1% of credit received

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.6 (Modeling Default Risk), p.79
raw_chunk_text:
Ultimately, skews became so steep that the hedge funds made money
risk-free the other way round—through put spreads. A popular trade for

rule_text: Execute put spread arbitrage when skew creates a pricing opportunity where a multi-leg put spread can be traded at zero cost or for a net credit, locking in risk-free profit from the volatility surface mispricing.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility skew steepness: slope > 0.10
Put spread net premium received: >= 0% (at-zero or credit)
Credit default swap spread component: < 200 bps
Payoff floor verified: lowest payoff >= 0 at 90% confidence
exit_conditions: Spread trades at debit: close position
Skew flattens to < 7 bps: monitor and exit if trend continues
Time decay: close if 50% of premium eroded without directional move

rule_text: Execute put spread arbitrage when skew creates a pricing opportunity where a multi-leg put spread can be traded at zero cost or for a net credit, locking in risk-free profit from the volatility surface mispricing.

Position involves strike-sensitive payoff (digital, barrier, or deep OTM)
Notional exposure > 5M USD equivalent
exit_conditions: Skew returns to baseline levels
Position closed or hedged

rule_text: A trader should exploit pricing discrepancies between counterparties who use different volatility models (local vs. stochastic) when valuing complex derivatives, because sellers using inadequate models systematically underprice and will capture market share despite fundamental mispricing.
entry_conditions: Detect that dealer is using local volatility model for digital cliquet valuation
Confirm dealer has accumulated significant digital cliquet inventory on book
Verify stochastic volatility model would price 15%+ higher
exit_conditions: Dealer reprices using corrected volatility model
Inventory normalized or hedged
Time decay reduces edge below 10%

rule_text: A trader should exploit pricing discrepancies between counterparties who use different volatility models (local vs. stochastic) when valuing complex derivatives, because sellers using inadequate models systematically underprice and will capture market share despite fundamental mispricing.
entry_conditions: Identify discrepancy between local volatility and forward skew assumptions in dealer quotes
Obtain competing quotes showing 8-12% valuation spread attributable to model choice
Confirm dealer using inadequate model has market share/competitive pressure
exit_conditions: Valuation convergence to stochastic volatility model level
Dealer stops quoting or reprices
Edge decays below 6%

rule_text: A trader should exploit pricing discrepancies between counterparties who use different volatility models (local vs. stochastic) when valuing complex derivatives, because sellers using inadequate models systematically underprice and will capture market share despite fundamental mispricing.
entry_conditions: Quote digital cliquet from two dealers; compare valuations
Model choice difference detectable in price (>5% spread)
Dealer using simpler model (local vol) prices lower
exit_conditions: Valuation spread narrows below 3%

Calendar time decay of 30 days

rule_text: A trader should reject or significantly mark up exotic option valuations when the pricing error magnitude exceeds a multiple of typical deal margins, especially when competitors are pricing below a sound valuation model.
entry_conditions: Exotic option (e.g., digital cliquet, forward-starting digital) pricing request received
Estimated pricing error magnitude calculated using sound valuation model
Competitor pricing observed below sound model valuation
exit_conditions: Exit: Decline deal entirely or demand compensation for model risk premium equal to 2x typical margin

rule_text: A trader should reject or significantly mark up exotic option valuations when the pricing error magnitude exceeds a multiple of typical deal margins, especially when competitors are pricing below a sound valuation model.

rule_text: A trader should apply a gap risk premium adjustment to barrier option valuations when continuous trading cannot be guaranteed, because discrete execution allows prices to skip through barrier levels rather than execute exactly at them.
entry_conditions: Position involves short barrier options (seller perspective)
Underlying asset exhibits overnight gaps or low intraday liquidity near barrier
Time to barrier expiry is less than 30 days (higher gap probability)
exit_conditions: Barrier is hit and option knocks out
Underlying gaps through barrier (execute at market, accept slippage)

rule_text: A trader should apply a gap risk premium adjustment to barrier option valuations when continuous trading cannot be guaranteed, because discrete execution allows prices to skip through barrier levels rather than execute exactly at them.
entry_conditions: Position involves short barrier options on highly liquid underliers
Underlying has low historical gap frequency and tight bid-ask spreads
Barrier is relatively far from current price (>5% away)
exit_conditions: Barrier level is breached at any price
Option knock-out occurs

rule_text: Rebalance dynamic hedges by rolling option strikes upward when the underlying asset reaches prior strike levels, capturing the gamma profit differential between old and new strikes.

Calculate gamma profit: 2(∂C/∂K) × ΔK
Gamma profit > 0 (positive convexity exists)

parameters.rebalance_frequency: Every time S reaches prior K (continuous monitoring)

exit_conditions: Stock price reverses below K
Lookback option expires worthless (S never reaches subsequent strikes)

rule_text: Rebalance dynamic hedges by rolling option strikes upward when the underlying asset reaches prior strike levels, capturing the gamma profit differential between old and new strikes.

rule_text: Construct a portfolio hedge where long and short option positions offset each other's payoff across barrier levels, minimizing net exposure under varying market conditions.
entry_conditions: Position is long an exotic option (e.g., down-and-out call)
Identify barrier level B where payoff offset occurs
Construct static hedge: long call at K, short put at K where B=K
exit_conditions: Cumulative payoff under all scenarios exceeds 5% of notional
Barrier is breached
Maturity reached

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.10, p.210 (Figure 10.4 caption and surrounding discussion on reverse cliquets)
rule_text: A trader should increase long volatility exposure (or reduce short volatility exposure) when forward skew becomes more negative, because negative skew increases the value of downside-protection structures.
entry_conditions: Forward skew (90th percentile put IV / ATM IV) < -0.15
Skew has moved lower by at least -0.05 in the past 5 trading days
Current skew is in the bottom quartile of the last 60 days
exit_conditions: Skew reverts above -0.10
Position mark-to-market loss exceeds -8%
Holding period exceeds 30 calendar days

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.10, p.210 (Figure 10.4 caption and surrounding discussion on reverse cliquets)
rule_text: A trader should increase long volatility exposure (or reduce short volatility exposure) when forward skew becomes more negative, because negative skew increases the value of downside-protection structures.
entry_conditions: Forward skew < 20-day moving average of skew by at least -0.08
Current skew level < -0.10
Skew slope (skew change over 5 days) is negative
exit_conditions: Skew crosses back above 20-day moving average
Position mark-to-market loss exceeds -12%
Holding period exceeds 20 calendar days

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.10, p.210 (Figure 10.4 caption and surrounding discussion on reverse cliquets)
rule_text: A trader should increase long volatility exposure (or reduce short volatility exposure) when forward skew becomes more negative, because negative skew increases the value of downside-protection structures.
entry_conditions: Forward skew declines by -0.03 or more in a single day
Current skew < -0.05
No holding period restriction on existing positions
exit_conditions: Skew improves (becomes less negative) by +0.04 in a single day
Position mark-to-market loss exceeds -15%
Holding period exceeds 10 calendar days

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Chapter 10 (Exotic Cliquets), p.129

and the lower the value of the structure. Our previous
rule_text: A trader should reduce exposure to structured products with embedded downside puts when volatility skew becomes more negative, because negative skew increases put value and reduces overall structure value under stochastic volatility assumptions.
entry_conditions: Position in capped-coupon exotic structure (e.g., Napoleon) already established
Volatility skew measure (e.g., 25-delta put IV minus ATM IV) becomes more negative than -0.5%
exit_conditions: Exit full position when skew falls below -0.5%
Re-enter only when skew returns to neutral or positive

exit_conditions: Reduce position by 50% when skew falls below -1.0%

rule_text: Before trading an unfamiliar or poorly understood derivative structure, a trader should systematically analyze how pricing sensitivity varies across different modeling assumptions rather than relying on intuition alone.
entry_conditions: Structure familiarity score ≤ 3 (on 1-10 scale)
Sensitivity analysis completed across ≥ 3 distinct models
Model pricing divergence ≤ 2% across all Greeks (delta, vega, gamma)
Cross-effects (e.g., vega decline under adverse spot moves) quantified and understood
exit_conditions: Model divergence exceeds threshold during position life
Structure behavior contradicts pre-trade sensitivity assumptions

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2, pp.13-210
raw_chunk_text:
underpricedinthelocalvolatilitymodelrelativetostochasticvolatilityand
in this case, the underpricing of volatility convexity may be the dominant
effect. The Napoleon structure also has the feature tha
rule_text: Before trading an unfamiliar or poorly understood derivative structure, a trader should systematically analyze how pricing sensitivity varies across different modeling assumptions rather than relying on intuition alone.
entry_conditions: Structure familiarity score ≤ 5 (on 1-10 scale)
Sensitivity analysis completed across ≥ 2 distinct models (local vol vs stochastic vol)
Model pricing divergence ≤ 5% in primary Greeks (vega exposure)
Direction of cross-effects (vol convexity, coupon floor impact) consistent across models
exit_conditions: Model divergence exceeds 5% during position life
Cross-effect behavior (e.g., vega collapse under stress) occurs unexpectedly

rule_text: Before trading an unfamiliar or poorly understood derivative structure, a trader should systematically analyze how pricing sensitivity varies across different modeling assumptions rather than relying on intuition alone.
entry_conditions: Structure familiarity score ≥ 6 (on 1-10 scale)
Primary sensitivity analysis in best-fit model completed
Intuition-based structural understanding confirmed by at least 1 alternative model check
Key cross-effects mapped even if secondary effects show >10% divergence
exit_conditions: Unexpected coupon floor hit or vega collapse
Dominant pricing mechanism shifts between model assumptions

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2, pp.13-210 (Napoleon structure and independent increment technique discussion)
raw_chunk_text:
Napoleon structure was popular were smart enough to realize that local
volatility models were inappropriate because, as we keep noting, forward
skewsaretooflat.However,themostcommonvaluationtechniquew
rule_text: A trader should exploit mispricing in structured products when the valuation method used by the market systematically underprices volatility convexity due to deterministic volatility assumptions.
entry_conditions: Identify market participant using independent increment valuation technique
Measure forward volatility convexity mispricing as percentage of 1-month ATM vol
Mispricing > 3.0% of current implied volatility level
Verify volatility term structure is upward sloping (supports convexity underpricing)
exit_conditions: Mispricing shrinks below 1.5% of implied vol
Market switches from independent increment to stochastic volatility model
Time decay erodes more than 50% of position PnL gain

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2, pp.13-210 (Napoleon structure and independent increment technique discussion)

Napoleon structure was popular were smart enough to realize that local
volatility models were inappropriate because, as we keep noting, forward
skewsaretooflat.However,themostcommonvaluationtechniquew
rule_text: A trader should exploit mispricing in structured products when the valuation method used by the market systematically underprices volatility convexity due to deterministic volatility assumptions.
entry_conditions: Forward skew is flatter than historical/realized skew levels
Forward volatility shows minimal term structure volatility
Mispricing > 1.5% of current implied volatility level
Options market is quoting consistent levels (low bid-ask relative to vol)
exit_conditions: Mispricing shrinks below 0.75% of implied vol
Time decay erodes more than 30% of position PnL gain
Realized convexity diverges >2 std devs from expected model output

source_citation: The Volatility Surface, Jim Gatheral, Ch.2, pp.13-210 (Napoleon structure and independent increment technique discussion)

rule_text: A trader should exploit mispricing in structured products when the valuation method used by the market systematically underprices volatility convexity due to deterministic volatility assumptions.
entry_conditions: Forward volatility term structure has near-zero curvature (deterministic pattern detected)
Bid-ask spread < 1% of mid price (sufficient liquidity)
Any measurable mispricing > 0.5% of implied vol
exit_conditions: Mispricing shrinks below 0.25% or turns negative
Stop loss at -15% of position notional
Time decay erodes more than 20% of position PnL gain
Hold maximum 2 weeks if no convexity realization occurs

rule_text: A trader should sell variance swaps (or realized volatility) when implied volatility reaches elevated levels relative to expected realized volatility, because variance swaps eliminate path dependency and delta-hedging labor while capturing pure realized vol exposure.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility percentile rank >= 75th percentile (90-day lookback)
Confirmed structural dealer short vega exposure (via dealer positioning data or VIX term structure backwardation)
Realized volatility expectation < Implied volatility by minimum 200bps
exit_conditions: IV percentile falls below 60th percentile
Realized vol realized exceeds implied vol by 50bps
Position duration reaches 30 days

rule_text: A trader should sell variance swaps (or realized volatility) when implied volatility reaches elevated levels relative to expected realized volatility, because variance swaps eliminate path dependency and delta-hedging labor while capturing pure realized vol exposure.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility percentile rank between 60th and 70th percentile (60-day lookback)
Realized volatility expectation < Implied volatility by minimum 100bps
No requirement for explicit dealer positioning confirmation (use as confirmation only)
exit_conditions: IV percentile falls below 50th percentile
Realized vol exceeds implied vol
Position duration reaches 45 days

rule_text: A trader should hedge short option positions by rebalancing when quadratic variation increases by a threshold amount, profiting when realized volatility is below implied volatility at inception.
entry_conditions: Short option position established at implied volatility level IV_0
Cumulative quadratic variation increases by 0.5% of initial variance estimate
exit_conditions: Option expires or is closed
Realized volatility exceeds implied volatility + 2%
